title: Tries
summary: When a problem says **prefix**, **starts with**, or **dictionary lookup**, it is telling you to build a Trie — a tree where every edge is a character and every root-to-node path is a prefix. You already have some Trie reps, so this module locks in a bug-free template you can type from muscle memory, plus the two clever variants that decide harder OAs: **word search on a board** and the **XOR bit-trie**.

notes.heading: When a Trie is the tell
notes.body:
Most data-structure choices in an OA are announced by a keyword, and the Trie's keyword is the loudest of all: **prefix**. Train yourself to hear it in every disguise:

- The statement mentions **prefixes**, **"starts with"**, **dictionary / word lookup**, or **auto-complete**.
- You must answer **many string queries against one fixed set of words**. A Trie amortizes shared prefixes, so each lookup costs $O(L)$ — the *word's* length — no matter whether the set holds ten words or a hundred thousand.
- You're doing **DFS on a letter grid while matching a word list** (Word Search II). A Trie lets one board walk prune *every* word sharing a dead prefix at once, instead of re-searching per word.
- The problem asks to **maximize or minimize XOR over pairs**, or make greedy bit-by-bit decisions. That's a **binary trie built over the bits** of each number — strings of 0s and 1s instead of letters.

> [!KEY]
> A Trie earns its keep exactly when **prefixes matter**. A hash set answers "is this whole word present?" in $O(L)$ too — the Trie's superpower is answering questions about *partial* words: counts under a prefix, shortest root, wildcard branching, all-words-sharing-this-path.

Just as important is knowing when **not** to reach for it:

- Single-string pattern matching → that's KMP/Z territory, not a Trie.
- If prefixes never matter and you only test whole-word membership, an `unordered_set<string>` is simpler, just as fast, and less code to get wrong.

> [!EXAM]
> In a timed OA, reaching for a Trie when a hashset suffices costs you ten minutes of typing and a memory-heavy solution. Ask one question before building: *"do I ever query something shorter than a whole word?"* If no — hashset. If yes — Trie, no hesitation.

notes.heading: Anatomy: edges are characters, paths are prefixes
The extras harder problems bolt on
notes.body:
The base template is rarely the whole answer. Harder ladder problems each add **one small field to the node** — recognize which field the problem is asking for:

- **`passCount`** — increment on every node you *pass through* during insert. Now "how many words start with prefix $p$?" is a single $O(|p|)$ walk that reads one counter. This is exactly Implement Trie II and the heart of Map Sum Pairs (store a value instead of a count and sum over the subtree — or, cleaner, add the *delta* to every node on the path when a key is overwritten).
- **`endCount`** — increment at the terminal node, so duplicates are counted and `erase` becomes "walk down, decrement counters." No node deletion needed for correctness.
- **`string word`** on the terminal node — store the whole word there. When a DFS lands on the node, it can emit the word without reconstructing the path. This is the trick that makes Word Search II clean.
- **map children** — swap `child[26]` for `unordered_map<char, TrieNode*>` when the alphabet is Unicode, digits + letters, or unknown. Same logic, different lookup.

> [!NOTE]
> All of these are *additive*: the insert/walk skeleton never changes. When you practice, type the base template first, then splice in the one extra field the problem needs. That habit keeps your OA code short and your bugs local.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Replace Words wants each word replaced by its **shortest** dictionary root. Insert all roots; for each sentence word, walk letter by letter and **stop at the first node with `isEnd == true`** — the walk order guarantees the shortest root wins. One extra `if` inside the walk; nothing else changes.

notes.heading: The binary trie: XOR problems in disguise
Pitfalls, memory, and how to narrate
notes.body:
The Trie template is short, which means every bug is one of the same five. Check them like a pre-flight list:

- **`isEnd` off-by-one.** `search("app")` must be false when only `"apple"` was inserted. `startsWith` says yes; `search` demands `isEnd`.
- **`ch - 'a'` assumes lowercase.** Confirm the alphabet before typing. Uppercase, digits, or mixed input → widen the array or switch to a map — and *say the assumption out loud* in an interview.
- **Empty string.** Inserting `""` just sets `root->isEnd`. Decide whether the problem allows it before your loops run zero times by accident.
- **XOR trie: MSB-first, fixed width.** Both halves matter (see the previous section).
- **Word Search II: restore and dedupe.** Forgetting `board[i][j] = ch` after the DFS corrupts every later search; forgetting to clear `node->word` emits duplicates. These two are *the* classic bugs of the hardest Trie problem.

> [!WARNING]
> In C++, every `new TrieNode()` leaks unless freed. For an OA nobody cares — but if an interviewer asks, mention a destructor that DFS-deletes children, or `unique_ptr<TrieNode> child[26]`. Knowing the answer exists is usually enough.

> [!EXAM]
> Narration script for Microsoft/Flipkart rounds: *"The keyword is **prefix**, so I'll use a Trie — insert and lookup are O(word length). For the board problem I'll walk the Trie and the grid together so shared prefixes are explored once, and I'll prune dead Trie branches as words are found."* Naming the pattern and its complexity in the first thirty seconds signals you've seen it before — that's points before you write a line.

problems.name: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
problems.note: The canonical `insert`/`search`/`startsWith` — retype it until your fingers produce it without thinking.

problems.name: Longest Common Prefix
problems.note: Solvable without a Trie, but do it *with* one: the LCP is the path before the first branch or `isEnd`.

problems.name: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
problems.note: On `.` branch a DFS into all 26 children; on a normal letter walk the single edge.

problems.name: Replace Words
problems.note: Insert the roots, then walk each word and stop at the **first** `isEnd` — shortest root wins by walk order.

problems.name: Map Sum Pairs
problems.note: Store values on nodes and sum over the prefix subtree (or push the delta down the path on insert).

problems.name: Search Suggestions System
problems.note: After each typed char, DFS from the prefix node for the 3 lexicographically smallest words.

problems.name: Word Break
problems.note: DP over positions that pairs beautifully with a Trie: one walk from `i` tests all dictionary prefixes.

problems.name: Longest Word in Dictionary
problems.note: A word is buildable only if **every** prefix is also a word — DFS only through `isEnd` nodes.

problems.name: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
problems.note: The flagship binary-trie greedy: insert bits MSB-first, then grab the opposite bit at every level.

problems.name: Word Search II
problems.note: One board DFS walking the Trie in lockstep — store the word on the node, clear it to dedupe, prune dead leaves.

problems.name: Concatenated Words
problems.note: Trie + DP: sort by length, then test whether each word splits into ≥2 shorter dictionary words.

problems.name: Palindrome Pairs
problems.note: Reversed-word Trie plus palindrome checks on the leftover halves — genuinely tricky, budget real time.

problems.name: Maximum XOR With an Element From Array
problems.note: Offline queries: sort by limit, insert numbers into the bit-trie only as the limit sweep passes them.

deepdives.q: **Word Search II** — given a board of letters and a list of words, return every word that can be traced through adjacent cells, without reusing a cell within one word.
**Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array** — find the maximum value of `a XOR b` over all pairs of elements in an integer array.
**Design Add and Search Words Data Structure** — support `addWord(word)` and `search(query)`, where the query may contain `.` characters, each matching any single letter.
**Concatenated Words** — given a list of distinct words, return every word that can be formed by concatenating **at least two** other (shorter) words from the same list.

quiz.q: An OA gives you $10^5$ dictionary words and $10^5$ queries, each asking *"how many words start with prefix $p$?"* Which approach answers each query in $O(|p|)$, independent of the number of words?
options: An `unordered_set` of all words — check membership of each prefix
Sort the words and binary-search the prefix range for every query
A Trie whose nodes carry a `passCount` incremented on every insert step
A suffix array built over the concatenation of all words
quiz.explanation: Walking the Trie along $p$ and reading `passCount` at the final node is exactly $O(|p|)$ — the word count never appears in the bound. The hashset can only test whole-word membership, not count prefix matches. Sorting + binary search *does* work but costs $O(|p| \log n)$ per query and can't absorb insertions cheaply. A suffix array answers substring questions — massive overkill for prefixes.

quiz.q: You "simplify" the template so that `search` returns `walk(w) != nullptr`. After `insert("apple")`, which call now gives a **wrong** answer?
options: None; the two functions are equivalent
quiz.explanation: Dropping the `isEnd` check turns `search` into `startsWith`: the node for "app" exists (it's on the path to "apple"), so `search("app")` wrongly reports the word present. `search("apple")` still works — the node exists — which is exactly why this bug slips through lazy testing. Always test a strict prefix of an inserted word.

quiz.q: In `queryMaxXor` you rewrite the loop as `for (int b = 0; b <= BITS; ++b)` (LSB first) while `insert` still runs MSB first. What happens?
options: It only fails when the array contains negative numbers
It still returns the right answer, just with worse cache behavior
The walk descends levels keyed by the wrong bits, so the traversal itself is broken — not just the greedy order
quiz.explanation: Trie level $d$ stores bit $\text{BITS}-d$ because insert went MSB-first. Querying LSB-first compares bit 0 against the level that stores bit 30, so the pointer walk follows edges that mean something else entirely — garbage answers, not merely suboptimal ones. And even with a matching LSB-first insert, the greedy would be wrong: low bits would be locked in before high bits, inverting the priority $2^b > 2^{b-1} + \dots + 1$.

quiz.q: You insert $n$ lowercase words with $C$ total characters into an array-child Trie. What are the build time and worst-case space?
quiz.explanation: Each inserted character does $O(1)$ work (index into `child`, maybe allocate), so building is $O(C)$ — the 26 never multiplies *time* because array indexing is constant. It multiplies *space*: in the worst case (no shared prefixes) you allocate up to $C$ nodes, each carrying a 26-pointer array, hence $O(26 \cdot C)$. Sorting bounds ($n \log n$) have nothing to do with Tries.

quiz.q: Trace it: `insert("app"); insert("apple");` then call `search("appl")` and `startsWith("appl")`. What do they return, in that order?
quiz.explanation: The node for "appl" exists — it sits on the path to "apple" — so `startsWith("appl")` is true. But no inserted word *ends* there: `isEnd` is true at "app" and at "apple", not in between, so `search("appl")` is false. Option D is impossible in any correct Trie: `search(x)` true forces `startsWith(x)` true, since the exact word is its own prefix.

quiz.q: In your Word Search II DFS you delete the last line, `b[i][j] = ch;` (the restore after exploring neighbors). What's the symptom?
options: Infinite recursion, since cells are never marked visited
Cells stay `'#'` forever, so later paths are blocked and valid words go missing from the output
The same word appears multiple times in the output
Undefined behavior from writing outside the board
quiz.explanation: The `'#'` write is the *visited* mark; the restore is what un-marks it when the DFS backtracks. Without the restore, every explored cell remains a permanent wall, so subsequent starting cells and branches see a corrupted board and miss real words. Marking still happens, so there's no infinite recursion — and duplicates are the symptom of the *other* classic bug, forgetting to clear `node->word`.

quiz.q: For Design Add and Search Words, what is the worst-case time for one `search` on a query of length $L$ that is **all dots**, against an adversarially full Trie?
options: $O(26^L)$ — every dot can branch into up to 26 children
quiz.explanation: Each `.` multiplies the live branches by up to 26, and $L$ dots compound: $26^L$ paths in the worst case. The $O(L)$ guarantee only holds for wildcard-free queries, where each step follows exactly one child. $O(26L)$ is the tempting trap — it assumes the branches don't *nest*, but each branch recurses into its own subtree. In practice most children are null, so real inputs stay fast; the bound is about the adversarial case.

m07-bit-greedy.caption: Bit-trie holding 3 (`011`) and 5 (`101`); querying **x = 6 (`110`)** walks MSB→LSB, grabbing the opposite bit whenever that child exists. Two grabs succeed, one is forced — answer `101` = 5 = 6 XOR 3.

m07-wildcard-branch.caption: `search("a.t")`: after the fixed letter `a`, the `.` fans the DFS into **every existing child** (n, p, r). Two branches complete with `t` (`"ant"`, `"art"`); one dies (`p` has no `t` child). Any surviving branch makes the whole search true.
